attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman attends the follow-up clinic six weeks after undergoing an abdominoplasty after previous weight loss surgery. She has made a good recovery from the procedure, but complains of persistent abdominal discomfort and swelling. The patient denies any history of trauma or unusual activities post-surgery.

On examination there is an asymmetrical swelling and tenderness along the lower abdomen, with a palpable mass in the left lower quadrant. There are no signs of ecchymosis, infection, or any change in size of the mass when the patient is asked to cough.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for her current problem?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely explanation for this patient's current problem is a wound seroma. Seroma formation is a common complication of abdominoplasty and other similar procedures there is a potential space between the various layers of tissue dissection. Such collections may occur in some 10% of patients after abdominoplasty. Fluid can accumulate in this space and present as a persistent swelling with discomfort. A palpable fluid collection is often evident. Seromas can be managed with aspiration or drainage procedures if they are large or symptomatic, but otherwise can be left alone and allowed to be reabsorbed.

The occurrence of seroma after abdominoplasty emphasises the importance of implementing preventive measures such as adequate drainage, meticulous tissue dissection, and adherence to postoperative care protocols to minimise fluid accumulation and optimise patient recovery and satisfaction.

Haematoma formation is a potential complication of abdominoplasty, but it is not the most likely explanation for the patient's symptoms in the provided clinical scenario. Haematoma formation typically presents with localised swelling, bruising, and tenderness at the surgical site. In the scenario provided, there is no mention of significant bruising or discoloration, which would be more characteristic of haematoma formation.

Deep wound dehiscence refers to the partial or complete separation of the deep layers of the surgical incision. It can occur due to factors such as poor wound healing, infection, or excessive tension on the incision site. Patients may present with a visible gap in the incision, along with pain and increased drainage.

An umbilical hernia is a protrusion of abdominal contents through a weakness or defect near the umbilicus. It can develop as a complication of abdominoplasty if the sutures used to secure the umbilicus are not properly placed or if there is underlying weakness in the abdominal wall. Symptoms include a visible bulge or swelling at the umbilicus, discomfort, and possibly nausea or vomiting if the hernia becomes incarcerated or strangulated. The swelling would become more noticeable with increased intra-abdominal pressure (coughing).

Abdominal wall laxity can occur as a result of inadequate tissue tightening during abdominoplasty or due to factors such as weight gain or pregnancy after surgery. Patients may complain of a recurrence of abdominal protrusion or bulging, despite having undergone abdominoplasty.
learningPoints: Seroma formation is a well recognised complication following dissection of tissue layers.
references: Ho W, Jones CD, Pitt E, Hallam MJ. Meta-analysis on the comparative efficacy of drains, progressive tension sutures and subscarpal fat preservation in reducing complications of abdominoplasty. J Plast Reconstr Aesthet Surg. 2020 May;73(5):828-840. doi: 10.1016/j.bjps.2019.11.066. Epub 2020 Feb 1. PMID: 32057746.
Salari  N, Fatahi  B, Bartina Y, et al. The Global Prevalence of Seroma After Abdominoplasty: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Asthetic Plast Surg. 2021; 45:2821-36.
